questions.question:
A database of research articles in a journal uses the following schema.
(VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE, TITLE, YEAR, PRICE)
The primary key is (VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE) and the following functional dependencies exist in the schema:
(VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE) -> TITLE
(VOLUME, NUMBER) -> YEAR
(VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE) -> PRICE
The database is redesigned to use the following schemas:
(VOLUME, NUMBER, YEAR)
(VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE, TITLE, PRICE)
Which is the weakest normal form that the new design satisfies, but the old design does not?

A. 1NF
B. 2NF
C. 3NF
D. BCNF
questions.to_find: The weakest normal form satisfied by the new schema design but violated by the original schema

understand.plain: The original schema stores information about journal articles. The primary key is the combination (VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE). However, YEAR depends only on (VOLUME, NUMBER), which is a proper subset of the primary key — this is called a partial dependency, which violates 2NF. The redesign splits the schema to eliminate this partial dependency. We need to find the weakest normal form the new design satisfies but the old does not.

keywords.explain: A non-key attribute depends on only part of the composite primary key, not the whole key.
keywords.example: If PK = (A,B) and C -> A alone determines D, then D partially depends on the PK.

keywords.explain: A relation is in 2NF if it is in 1NF and every non-prime attribute is fully functionally dependent on every candidate key (no partial dependencies).
keywords.example: If PK=(A,B) and D depends on A alone (partial), the relation is NOT in 2NF.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 200' width='520' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='40' class='no' rx='5'/><text x='260' y='36' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>Original: YEAR depends only on (VOLUME, NUMBER) — partial dep!</text><rect x='10' y='60' width='240' height='60' class='ok' rx='5'/><text x='130' y='85' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' fill='white' font-weight='700'>(VOLUME, NUMBER, YEAR)</text><text x='130' y='108' class='sub' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>No partial deps — 2NF OK</text><rect x='270' y='60' width='240' height='60' class='ok' rx='5'/><text x='390' y='80' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' fill='white' font-weight='700'>(VOL, NUM, STARTPAGE,</text><text x='390' y='98' class='sub' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>ENDPAGE, TITLE, PRICE)</text><text x='390' y='116' class='sub' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>No partial deps — 2NF OK</text><text x='260' y='155' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>New design satisfies 2NF; old design did NOT</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Original schema has a partial dependency: YEAR depends on (VOLUME, NUMBER) which is only part of the full PK (VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE). Redesigned schemas each have full functional dependencies, achieving 2NF.

given.aim: Determine which normal form the new (split) schema satisfies but the original schema violates

terms.example: All four attributes together uniquely identify an article.
terms.connects: Any non-prime attribute must depend on the entire PK for 2NF compliance.

terms.meaning: YEAR is determined by just (VOLUME, NUMBER), not the full PK
terms.example: A partial dependency: YEAR depends on a proper subset of the PK.
terms.connects: This violates 2NF in the original schema.

terms.meaning: (VOLUME, NUMBER, YEAR) and (VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE, TITLE, PRICE)
terms.example: First schema: PK=(VOLUME,NUMBER); YEAR fully depends on it. Second schema: PK=(VOLUME,NUMBER,STARTPAGE,ENDPAGE); TITLE and PRICE fully depend on it.
terms.connects: Both redesigned schemas are free of partial dependencies, so they satisfy 2NF.

given.plan: Check whether the original schema is in 1NF, 2NF, 3NF, and BCNF by examining FDs against the primary key. Since (VOLUME, NUMBER) -> YEAR is a partial dependency (YEAR depends on a proper subset of the PK), the original schema violates 2NF. The redesign removes this partial dependency, so the new schemas satisfy 2NF (and higher). The weakest normal form newly satisfied is 2NF.

steps.title: Check original schema for partial dependencies
steps.formula_raw: 2NF: No non-prime attribute partially depends on any candidate key
steps.apply:
PK = (VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE). Non-prime attributes: TITLE, YEAR, PRICE.
- TITLE depends on full PK: OK
- PRICE depends on full PK: OK
- YEAR depends on (VOLUME, NUMBER) alone — this is a PROPER SUBSET of the PK.
This is a partial dependency. Hence the original schema is NOT in 2NF.
steps.note: The original schema is in 1NF but fails 2NF because of the partial dependency YEAR -> (VOLUME, NUMBER).

steps.title: Check redesigned schemas for partial dependencies
steps.formula_raw: Full functional dependency: X -> Y where removing any attribute from X breaks the FD
steps.apply:
Schema 1: (VOLUME, NUMBER, YEAR) with PK=(VOLUME, NUMBER). YEAR depends on full PK. No partial deps. => In 2NF (and 3NF and BCNF).
Schema 2: (VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE, TITLE, PRICE) with PK=(VOLUME, NUMBER, STARTPAGE, ENDPAGE). TITLE and PRICE both depend on the full PK. No partial deps. => In 2NF (and 3NF and BCNF).
The new design satisfies 2NF; the old does NOT.
steps.note: The weakest normal form satisfied by the new design but not the old is 2NF.

solution.result: B. 2NF — The redesigned schemas satisfy 2NF (by eliminating the partial dependency YEAR -> {VOLUME, NUMBER}) while the original schema did not.

questions.formula_note: Second Normal Form (2NF): all non-prime attributes must be fully functionally dependent on every candidate key; partial dependencies on a subset of the primary key violate 2NF.

questions.question:
The following functional dependencies hold true for the relational schema R(V, W, X, Y, Z):
- V -> W
- VW -> X
- Y -> VX
- Y -> Z

Which of the following is an irreducible equivalent for this set of functional dependencies?

A. V -> W
   W -> X
   Y -> V
   Y -> Z

B. V -> W
   VW -> X
   Y -> VX
   Y -> Z

C. V -> W
   V -> X
   Y -> V
   Y -> Z

D. V -> W
   V -> X
   Y -> X
   Y -> Z
questions.answer: C. V -> W, V -> X, Y -> V, Y -> Z
questions.to_find: The irreducible (canonical) cover of the given set of functional dependencies

understand.plain: An irreducible (canonical) cover of a set of FDs has three properties: (1) the right-hand side of every FD has exactly one attribute, (2) no FD has an extraneous attribute on the left-hand side, and (3) no FD can be removed without changing the closure. We need to simplify the given FDs to find such a canonical form.

keywords.explain: A minimal equivalent set of FDs where: each RHS has one attribute, no extraneous LHS attribute exists, and no FD is redundant.
keywords.example: If {A->B, B->C, A->C}, the FD A->C is redundant (derivable from A->B, B->C), so the canonical cover is {A->B, B->C}.

keywords.explain: An attribute in the LHS of a FD that can be removed without changing what can be derived.
keywords.example: If VW->X and V->W, then W is extraneous in VW->X because V alone determines W, and from V we can get W and then X.

understand.visual_alt: Canonical cover derivation: VW->X simplifies to V->X (since V->W makes W extraneous); Y->VX splits into Y->V and Y->X, but Y->X is redundant via Y->V->X; final cover: {V->W, V->X, Y->V, Y->Z}.

given.aim: Find the irreducible (canonical) cover of the FD set {V->W, VW->X, Y->VX, Y->Z}

terms.example: Knowing V, we can determine W.
terms.connects: Since V->W, in VW->X the attribute W is extraneous on the LHS.

terms.meaning: V and W together determine X
terms.example: VW->X simplifies: since V->W, substitute to get V->X.
terms.connects: After simplification, this becomes V->X in the canonical cover.

terms.meaning: Y determines both V and X
terms.example: Split into Y->V and Y->X. But since Y->V and V->X, Y->X is derivable, making it redundant.
terms.connects: Only Y->V is needed in the canonical cover (Y->X follows transitively).

given.plan: Step 1: Decompose all RHS to single attributes. Step 2: Remove extraneous attributes from each LHS. Step 3: Remove any redundant FDs that can be derived from the others.

steps.title: Decompose RHS to single attributes
steps.formula_raw: Split A -> BC into A -> B and A -> C
steps.apply:
Given FDs:
1. V -> W
2. VW -> X
3. Y -> VX  =>  split into  Y -> V  and  Y -> X
4. Y -> Z
Working set: {V->W, VW->X, Y->V, Y->X, Y->Z}
steps.note: Every FD must have exactly one attribute on the RHS in a canonical cover.

steps.title: Remove extraneous attributes from LHS
steps.formula_raw: W is extraneous in VW->X if V+ (closure of V) contains X without W
steps.apply:
For VW -> X: Check if V alone determines X.
V+ = {V} then V->W gives {V,W} then VW->X gives {V,W,X}.
So V+ = {V,W,X,...}, meaning V alone determines X.
Therefore W is extraneous in VW->X. Replace VW->X with V->X.
Updated set: {V->W, V->X, Y->V, Y->X, Y->Z}
steps.note: After this step, all LHS attributes are necessary.

steps.title: Remove redundant FDs
steps.formula_raw: FD f is redundant if it can be derived from the remaining FDs
steps.apply:
Check if Y->X is redundant: Without Y->X, compute Y+ from {V->W, V->X, Y->V, Y->Z}.
Y+ = {Y} -> Y->V adds V -> {Y,V} -> V->X adds X -> {Y,V,X} -> V->W adds W -> {Y,V,W,X} -> Y->Z adds Z -> {Y,V,W,X,Z}.
Y+ contains X, so Y->X IS derivable without it. Remove Y->X.
Final canonical cover: {V->W, V->X, Y->V, Y->Z}

solution.result: C. {V->W, V->X, Y->V, Y->Z} — This is the irreducible (canonical) cover of the given FD set.

questions.formula_note: Canonical cover algorithm: (1) decompose RHS to singletons, (2) remove extraneous LHS attributes using attribute closure, (3) remove redundant FDs whose consequences are derivable from the remaining set.

questions.question:
Consider the following four relational schemas. For each schema, all non-trivial functional dependencies are listed. The bolded attributes are the respective primary keys.

Schema I: Registration(rollno, courses)
Field 'courses' is a set-valued attribute containing the set of courses a student has registered for. There are no non-trivial functional dependencies.

Schema II: Registration(rollno, courseid, email)
Non-trivial functional dependencies:
rollno -> email
rollno, courseid -> email
email -> rollno

Schema III: Registration(rollno, courseid, marks, grade)
Non-trivial functional dependencies:
rollno, courseid -> marks
marks -> grade

Schema IV: Registration(rollno, courseid, credit)
Non-trivial functional dependencies:
rollno, courseid -> credit
courseid -> credit

Which of the following options is correct?

A. Schema I is in 3NF but not BCNF
B. Schema II is in 3NF but not BCNF
C. Schema III is in 3NF but not BCNF
D. Schema IV is in 3NF but not BCNF
questions.answer: B. Schema II is in 3NF but not BCNF
questions.to_find: Which schema is in 3NF but not in BCNF

understand.plain: For a schema to be in 3NF but not BCNF, there must be a FD where the determinant is not a superkey but the dependent attribute is a prime attribute (part of a candidate key). Schema II has overlapping candidate keys: {rollno, courseid} and {email, courseid}. The FD email->rollno has email as a non-superkey determinant, but rollno is a prime attribute (part of CK1). This violates BCNF but satisfies 3NF. Schema I is not even in 1NF (set-valued attribute). Schema III has a transitive dependency through a non-prime attribute (violates 3NF). Schema IV has a partial dependency (violates 2NF).

keywords.explain: A relation is in BCNF if for every non-trivial FD X->Y, X is a superkey. Stricter than 3NF.
keywords.example: If A->B and A is not a superkey, BCNF is violated even if B is a prime attribute.

keywords.explain: A relation is in 3NF if for every non-trivial FD X->Y, either X is a superkey OR Y is a prime attribute (part of some candidate key).
keywords.example: email->rollno: email is not a superkey, but rollno IS a prime attribute (part of CK {rollno,courseid}), so 3NF is satisfied.

keywords.explain: An attribute that is part of at least one candidate key.
keywords.example: In Schema II with CKs {rollno,courseid} and {email,courseid}: rollno, courseid, and email are all prime attributes.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 220' width='500' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='225' height='90' class='no' rx='5'/><text x='122' y='32' class='tag' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>Schema I: NOT 1NF</text><text x='122' y='56' class='sub' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>Set-valued attribute</text><text x='122' y='76' class='sub' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>'courses' violates 1NF</text><rect x='265' y='10' width='225' height='90' class='ok' rx='5'/><text x='377' y='32' class='tag' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>Schema II: 3NF not BCNF</text><text x='377' y='56' class='sub' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>email->rollno: email not superkey</text><text x='377' y='76' class='sub' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>but rollno is prime (ANSWER)</text><rect x='10' y='120' width='225' height='90' class='no' rx='5'/><text x='122' y='142' class='tag' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>Schema III: NOT 3NF</text><text x='122' y='162' class='sub' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>marks->grade: transitive dep,</text><text x='122' y='182' class='sub' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>grade is non-prime</text><rect x='265' y='120' width='225' height='90' class='no' rx='5'/><text x='377' y='142' class='tag' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>Schema IV: NOT 2NF</text><text x='377' y='162' class='sub' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>courseid->credit: partial dep</text><text x='377' y='182' class='sub' text-anchor='middle' fill='white'>on subset of PK</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Schema I fails 1NF (set-valued attribute). Schema II is in 3NF but not BCNF due to email->rollno where email is not a superkey but rollno is prime. Schema III fails 3NF (transitive dependency marks->grade, grade is non-prime). Schema IV fails 2NF (partial dependency courseid->credit).

given.aim: Identify which schema is in 3NF but not in BCNF

terms.meaning: CK1 = {rollno, courseid} (PK as bolded), CK2 = {email, courseid} (since email->rollno and rollno,courseid->email implies email,courseid can determine rollno and hence entire schema)
terms.example: From email->rollno and rollno,courseid->email: email+courseid->rollno->email (circular), so {email,courseid} is also a CK.
terms.connects: Having two overlapping CKs means rollno, email, courseid are ALL prime attributes.

terms.meaning: email alone determines rollno, but email is not a superkey
terms.example: email is not a superkey (email alone cannot determine courseid). So email->rollno violates BCNF.
terms.connects: However, rollno IS a prime attribute (in CK1={rollno,courseid}), so 3NF is NOT violated by email->rollno.

terms.meaning: marks determines grade; marks is a non-prime, non-superkey attribute; grade is non-prime
terms.example: PK={rollno,courseid}. marks is not a key. grade is not in any CK. So marks->grade violates 3NF.
terms.connects: Schema III fails 3NF (not just BCNF), so it cannot be 'in 3NF but not BCNF'.

given.plan: Analyze each schema: check for 1NF (no set-valued attributes), 2NF (no partial deps on PK), 3NF (for every non-trivial X->Y, X is superkey OR Y is prime), BCNF (for every non-trivial X->Y, X is superkey). Identify which schema passes 3NF but fails BCNF.

steps.title: Analyze Schema I
steps.formula_raw: 1NF: all attribute values must be atomic
steps.apply: Schema I has 'courses' as a set-valued attribute. A set is not atomic. Therefore Schema I is NOT even in 1NF, let alone 3NF.
steps.note: Schema I fails 1NF immediately, so option A is wrong.

steps.title: Analyze Schema IV
steps.formula_raw: 2NF: no partial dependencies on primary key
steps.apply:
Schema IV: Registration(rollno, courseid, credit). PK = {rollno, courseid}.
FD: courseid -> credit. courseid is a PROPER SUBSET of PK, and credit is non-prime. This is a partial dependency.
Schema IV is NOT in 2NF (hence not in 3NF). Option D is wrong.
steps.note: Schema IV fails 2NF due to the partial dependency courseid->credit.

steps.title: Analyze Schema III
steps.formula_raw: 3NF: for every non-trivial X->Y, X is superkey OR Y is a prime attribute
steps.apply:
Schema III: Registration(rollno, courseid, marks, grade). PK = {rollno, courseid}.
FD: marks -> grade. marks is not a superkey. grade is NOT a prime attribute (not in any CK).
This violates 3NF (transitive dependency through a non-key attribute).
Schema III is NOT in 3NF. Option C is wrong.
steps.note: Schema III fails 3NF due to the transitive dependency marks->grade.

steps.title: Analyze Schema II
steps.formula_raw: 3NF: X->Y allowed if X is superkey OR Y is prime. BCNF: X->Y requires X to be superkey.
steps.apply:
Schema II: Registration(rollno, courseid, email). Bolded PK = {rollno, courseid}.
FDs: rollno->email, rollno,courseid->email, email->rollno.
Find all candidate keys:
- CK1 = {rollno, courseid} (given as PK)
- From email->rollno: if we have email and courseid, we can derive rollno (via email->rollno), then all other attributes. So CK2 = {email, courseid}.
Prime attributes: rollno (in CK1), courseid (in CK1 and CK2), email (in CK2). ALL attributes are prime!

Check 3NF for email->rollno:
- email is not a superkey (email alone doesn't determine courseid).
- But rollno IS a prime attribute (part of CK1).
- 3NF condition satisfied: Y (rollno) is prime.

Check BCNF for email->rollno:
- email is NOT a superkey.
- BCNF requires the determinant to be a superkey.
- BCNF VIOLATED.

So Schema II IS in 3NF but NOT in BCNF.
steps.note: Overlapping candidate keys are the classic scenario where 3NF and BCNF differ.

solution.result: B. Schema II is in 3NF but not BCNF — because email->rollno has a non-superkey determinant (email), but rollno is a prime attribute, satisfying 3NF while violating BCNF.

questions.formula_note: 3NF vs BCNF distinction: 3NF allows X->Y where Y is a prime attribute even if X is not a superkey; BCNF requires X to always be a superkey. Overlapping candidate keys create the gap between 3NF and BCNF.
